scenarios.title: Common Scenarios
scenarios.subtitle: Get specific guidance for real-life situations

police.title: Stopped by Police
police.description: Know your rights when questioned without reason
police.guidance: You have the right to remain silent and request legal counsel. Police must provide valid grounds for detention.
police.example: In a 2018 Delhi case, the court ruled in favor of a citizen illegally detained without proper documentation.
police.action: Request written documentation and contact a lawyer immediately.

discrimination.title: Workplace Discrimination
discrimination.description: Facing unfair treatment at school or work
discrimination.guidance: Discrimination based on religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth is illegal in India.
discrimination.example: A Mumbai company was fined for refusing employment based on caste in 2020.
discrimination.action: Document incidents and file a complaint with the National Human Rights Commission.

eviction.title: Landlord Issues
eviction.guidance: Landlords must provide written notice as per the rental agreement and cannot forcibly evict you.
eviction.example: In Bangalore, a court stayed eviction proceedings where proper notice wasn't given.
eviction.action: Demand written notice and consult a tenant rights lawyer.

harassment.title: Women Safety
harassment.right: Article 21 - Right to Live with Dignity
harassment.guidance: Sexual harassment is illegal. Women have the right to file FIR and seek police protection.
harassment.example: Supreme Court's Vishaka Guidelines mandate workplace safety committees.
harassment.action: File a police complaint and contact women's helpline 1091.

rights.title: Your Fundamental Rights
rights.subtitle: Know the 6 pillars of Indian democracy

equality.title: Right to Equality
equality.description: Prohibits discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth
equality.detailed: The Right to Equality ensures that all citizens are treated equally under the law without any discrimination. It prohibits the state from denying any person equality before the law or equal protection of laws. This right abolishes untouchability, prohibits discrimination in public places, and ensures equal opportunity in public employment.
provisions: Article 14: Equality before law and equal protection of laws
Article 15: Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth
Article 16: Equality of opportunity in matters of public employment
Article 17: Abolition of untouchability
Article 18: Abolition of titles except military and academic distinctions
equality.example: In the landmark case of Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala, the Supreme Court held that equality is part of the basic structure of the Constitution. In another case, a woman was denied employment based on her gender, and the court ruled in her favor citing Article 15 and 16.
equality.howTo: If you face discrimination, document all instances with dates and witnesses. File a complaint with the National Human Rights Commission or approach the courts directly. Legal aid is available for those who cannot afford legal representation.

freedom.title: Right to Freedom
freedom.description: Protects freedoms of speech, assembly, association, movement, residence, and profession
freedom.detailed: The Right to Freedom guarantees six fundamental freedoms to all citizens: freedom of speech and expression, assembly, association, movement, residence, and profession. It also provides protection against arrest and detention in certain cases and ensures certain rights to persons arrested under normal circumstances.
provisions: Article 19: Six freedoms - speech, assembly, association, movement, residence, and profession
Article 20: Protection in respect of conviction for offenses
Article 21: Protection of life and personal liberty
Article 21A: Right to education for children aged 6-14 years
Article 22: Protection against arrest and detention in certain cases
freedom.example: In the famous case of Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India, the Supreme Court expanded the scope of Article 21 to include the right to live with human dignity. Citizens regularly exercise their right to peaceful protest under Article 19, which has been upheld by courts numerous times.
freedom.howTo: To exercise these freedoms, ensure your actions are lawful and do not violate reasonable restrictions. If your rights are violated, approach the courts or human rights commissions. Document any instances of rights violations with evidence and seek legal counsel immediately.

exploitation.title: Right Against Exploitation
exploitation.description: Prohibits trafficking, forced labor, and child labor
exploitation.detailed: The Right Against Exploitation protects vulnerable sections of society from being exploited. It prohibits human trafficking and forced labor (begar), and prohibits the employment of children below 14 years in factories, mines, or other hazardous occupations.
provisions: Article 23: Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labor
Article 23(1): Traffic in human beings and begar (forced labor) are prohibited
Article 23(2): Contravention of this provision is an offense punishable by law
Article 24: Prohibition of employment of children in factories and hazardous occupations
Article 24: No child below 14 years can be employed in factories, mines, or other hazardous work
exploitation.example: In Bandhua Mukti Morcha v. Union of India, the Supreme Court directed the release of bonded laborers and their rehabilitation. In another case involving child labor in a factory, the employer was prosecuted under Article 24, and the children were provided education and rehabilitation.
exploitation.howTo: If you or someone you know is facing exploitation, immediately contact the local police, labor department, or childline (1098 for children). NGOs and legal aid organizations can provide assistance. Document evidence of exploitation and seek immediate legal intervention.

religion.title: Right to Freedom of Religion
religion.description: Ensures religious freedom and the right to manage religious institutions
religion.detailed: The Right to Freedom of Religion ensures that all persons are equally entitled to freedom of conscience and the right to freely profess, practice, and propagate religion. Subject to public order, morality, and health, every religious group has the right to establish and maintain institutions for religious and charitable purposes, manage its own affairs in matters of religion, and own property.
provisions: Article 25: Freedom of conscience and free profession, practice, and propagation of religion
Article 26: Freedom to manage religious affairs
Article 27: Freedom from payment of taxes for promotion of any particular religion
Article 28: Freedom from attending religious instruction or worship in educational institutions
State can regulate or restrict economic, financial, political activities associated with religious practices
religion.example: In the case of Commissioner, Hindu Religious Endowments v. Sri Lakshmindra Thirtha Swamiar, the Supreme Court held that the freedom of religion includes the right to manage religious institutions. In another case, forced religious conversion was struck down as violating Article 25.
religion.howTo: You can freely practice your religion within legal bounds. If you face discrimination or persecution based on religion, file a complaint with police or approach the courts. The National Human Rights Commission also addresses religious discrimination cases. Document all incidents and seek legal advice.

cultural.title: Cultural & Educational Rights
cultural.description: Protects rights of minorities to conserve culture and establish educational institutions
cultural.detailed: Cultural and Educational Rights protect the interests of minorities by ensuring their right to conserve their distinct language, script, and culture. Minorities have the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice. The State cannot discriminate against any educational institution on the ground that it is under the management of a minority group.
provisions: Article 29: Protection of interests of minorities - right to conserve distinct language, script, or culture
Article 29(2): No citizen shall be denied admission to educational institutions on grounds of religion, race, caste, or language
Article 30: Right of minorities to establish and administer educational institutions
Article 30(1): All minorities have the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice
Article 30(2): State shall not discriminate in granting aid to educational institutions on the ground of minority management
cultural.example: In St. Stephen's College v. University of Delhi, the Supreme Court upheld the right of minority institutions to have their own admission procedures. In another case, denial of admission to a student based on religion was struck down under Article 29(2).
cultural.howTo: If you're part of a minority community and wish to establish an educational institution, you have constitutional protection. If you face discrimination in admission or face threats to your cultural practices, approach the National Commission for Minorities or the courts with proper documentation.

remedies.title: Right to Constitutional Remedies
remedies.description: Guarantees the right to approach courts for enforcement of Fundamental Rights
remedies.detailed: The Right to Constitutional Remedies is considered the 'heart and soul' of the Constitution. It guarantees citizens the right to move the Supreme Court for the enforcement of their Fundamental Rights. The Supreme Court can issue directions, orders, or writs for the enforcement of these rights. This right itself is a Fundamental Right.
provisions: Article 32: Right to constitutional remedies including writs
Article 32(1): Right to move Supreme Court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights
Article 32(2): Supreme Court can issue writs - Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Quo Warranto, Certiorari
Article 226: High Courts can also issue writs but with wider jurisdiction
This right cannot be suspended except during Emergency
remedies.example: In Minerva Mills v. Union of India, the Supreme Court held that Article 32 is a basic feature of the Constitution and cannot be taken away. Countless citizens have used Article 32 to approach the Supreme Court when their rights were violated, from environmental issues to personal liberty cases.
remedies.howTo: If your Fundamental Rights are violated, you can directly file a writ petition in the Supreme Court under Article 32 or in the High Court under Article 226. Legal aid is available if you cannot afford a lawyer. Document all evidence of rights violation and consult a constitutional lawyer for proper guidance.

scenarioInput.title: Lawmate AI Assistant – Describe Your Situation
scenarioInput.subtitle: Type or speak your real-life legal scenario and get instant AI guidance in your language
scenarioInput.placeholder: Describe your situation here... (e.g., 'The police stopped me without reason')
scenarioInput.welcomeMessage: Welcome! Describe your legal situation below or use voice input. I'll respond with relevant rights, examples, and next steps.
scenarioInput.examplePrompt: Example: 'My employer is discriminating against me' or 'I was evicted without notice'

about.title: Our Mission
about.description: Lawmate's mission is to make legal knowledge accessible and multilingual — so every citizen of India can stand up for their rights.
about.subtitle: We believe every Indian deserves to understand and exercise their constitutional rights.
